name: Coruche and Erra Gravel Loop
description: This difficult 54.3-mile gravel biking loop from Coruche takes you through Portugal's cork heartland, with 1971 feet of elevation gain.
region_intro: Gravel biking around Coruche offers diverse landscapes, characterized by expansive cork oak forests and fertile agricultural fields. The region features rolling plains and dirt paths, often revealing distinctive red earth. The Sorraia River, a tributary of the Tagus, flows through the area, providing scenic routes along its banks. While generally flat, some routes include short, challenging climbs. Best gravel bike trails around Coruche The most popular gravel bike trail is Coruche and Erra Gravel Loop, a 54.3 miles (87.4 km) trail that takes 7 hours 43 minutes to complete. This difficult route winds through cork oak forests and agricultural lands. Another top favourite among local gravel bikers is Glória – Nó do Peso canal bridge loop from Coruche, a difficult 81.6 miles (131.4 km) path. This extensive route explores the wider region, offering varied terrain and views. Local gravel bikers also love the Magos Dam – Barragem de Magos loop from Marinhais, a 29.3 miles (47.1 km) trail leading through agricultural fields and open countryside, often completed in about 3 hours 7 minutes. Gravel biking around Coruche is defined by cork oak forests, fertile agricultural fields, and rolling plains. The network offers options for different ability levels, from moderate rides to more challenging, longer routes. The routes in Coruche are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 5.0 stars from a few reviews. More than 10 gravel bikers have used komoot to explore Coruche's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What types of landscapes will I encounter on gravel bike trails around Coruche?
A: Gravel biking around Coruche offers a diverse experience. You'll primarily ride through expansive &lt;strong&gt;cork oak forests&lt;/strong&gt; (montados) and pine tree fields, which are central to Portugal's cork industry. The routes also traverse fertile &lt;strong&gt;agricultural fields&lt;/strong&gt; (lezíria) along the Sorraia River, where Carolino rice is grown. Expect dirt paths with distinctive red earth, rolling plains, and olive groves, providing varied and scenic views.

Q: Are there any easy or moderate gravel bike routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, while many routes are challenging, there are options for different ability levels. For a moderate ride, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23771131" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Magos Dam – Barragem de Magos loop from Marinhais&lt;/a&gt;, which is about 47 km and takes around 3 hours. Another moderate option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23774228" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vale do Sorraia Gravel Route loop from Foros do Ameixial&lt;/a&gt;, a shorter 29.7 km ride. These routes generally lead through agricultural fields and open countryside, offering a less strenuous experience.

Q: What interesting sights or attractions can I see along the gravel bike trails in Coruche?
A: Coruche's trails often pass by significant natural and historical landmarks. You can explore the cork oak forests, visit the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/6630532" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sanctuary of Our Lady of the Castle of Coruche&lt;/a&gt; for panoramic views, or cycle near the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5728948" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Magos Dam&lt;/a&gt;. The region also features historical sites like the Roman-era Ponte da Corôa and a medieval aqueduct. The Sorraia River itself is a picturesque feature, with paths along its banks.

Q: Are there circular gravel bike trails available in Coruche?
A: Yes, many of the gravel bike routes in Coruche are designed as loops, allowing you to start and finish in the same location. Examples include the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e2332210730/coruche-and-erra-gravel-loop" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Coruche and Erra Gravel Loop&lt;/a&gt; and the extensive &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/32127593" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Glória – Nó do Peso canal bridge loop from Coruche&lt;/a&gt;. For a moderate option, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23774228" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Vale do Sorraia Gravel Route loop from Foros do Ameixial&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: When is the best time of year to go gravel biking in Coruche?
A: The best time to go gravel biking in Coruche is typically during the spring (March to May) and autumn (September to November). During these seasons, the weather is milder, with pleasant temperatures and less intense heat than the summer months. The landscapes are also particularly beautiful, with blooming wildflowers in spring and golden hues in autumn. Summers can be very hot, making longer rides challenging.

Q: What are the typical trail conditions for gravel biking in Coruche?
A: The gravel bike trails in Coruche primarily consist of dirt paths and unpaved roads. You'll encounter the distinctive red earth of the region, especially through the cork oak forests and agricultural fields. While generally well-maintained, conditions can vary with weather; expect some sandy or loose sections, especially in drier periods, and potentially muddy patches after rain. The terrain is mostly flat with some rolling plains, but a few routes include short, challenging climbs.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the gravel bike trails in Coruche?
A: While many outdoor areas in Portugal are dog-friendly, it's always best to check specific regulations for each trail or natural park. Generally, dogs on a leash are welcome on public paths. Be mindful of agricultural areas and livestock, and ensure your dog is well-behaved and doesn't disturb wildlife or local farming activities. Always carry water for your dog, especially on longer rides.

Q: Are there places to stop for food or drinks along the gravel routes?
A: Coruche and its surrounding villages offer various cafes and restaurants where you can refuel. While some routes might pass directly through or near small towns, others venture into more remote areas. It's advisable to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially on longer rides, and plan your stops in advance. Coruche itself has a good selection of eateries, and smaller villages like Marinhais or São José da Lamarosa may have local establishments.

Q: How can I reach Coruche by public transport for gravel biking?
A: Coruche is accessible by public transport, primarily by bus from major cities like Lisbon. There are regular bus services that connect Coruche to other towns in the Ribatejo region. While you can reach Coruche, transporting a gravel bike on public buses might require checking with the bus company beforehand regarding luggage policies and space availability. Alternatively, driving to Coruche and parking is a common option for cyclists.

Q: What do other gravel bikers enjoy the most about gravel biking in Coruche?
A: The area is highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 5.0 stars. Reviewers often praise the unique experience of cycling through the vast cork oak forests and the peaceful agricultural landscapes. The quiet roads and diverse terrain, offering a blend of flat sections and occasional climbs, are frequently highlighted as key attractions for an engaging gravel biking adventure.

Q: Where can I park my car when starting a gravel bike ride in Coruche?
A: In Coruche, you can typically find parking in the town center or near public facilities. For routes starting from smaller villages like Marinhais or São José da Lamarosa, there are usually public parking areas available. It's always a good idea to check the specific starting point of your chosen route on a map and look for designated parking zones or safe roadside parking options.

Q: Are there any specific events or organized rides for gravel biking in Coruche?
A: Coruche is increasingly recognized as a prime location for gravel biking and hosts events like the 'Gravel Meeting Ribatejo & Alentejo.' This semi-autonomous gravel challenge offers routes of varying distances through the region's diverse landscapes, designed to blend sport, leisure, nature, and adventure. Keep an eye on local cycling club calendars or event listings for upcoming organized rides.
tips: Sandy route, but you can still ride the route easily with a touring bike.
Lonely dirt road right next to the river.

Coruche is a peaceful town in Portugal, located between Ribatejo and Alentejo, known for its white traditional houses by the Sorraia River. Surrounded by fertile fields, it's a hub for agriculture, horse breeding, and cork production. The town features historic sites like the Roman-era Ponte da Corôa, a medieval aqueduct, and 17th-century churches. Nearby, the Agolada and Monte da Barca weirs are popular summer recreation spots.

faqs.question: What kind of terrain can I expect on the Loop from Coruche?
faqs.answer: The Loop from Coruche offers a varied gravel biking experience. You'll encounter a mix of mostly paved surfaces and dirt paths. These paths wind through agricultural land, iconic cork oak forests, and quiet rural roads. You'll also ride along a Dirt Road and a Dirt road next to the river, characteristic of the Ribatejo and Alentejo regions.

faqs.question: Is the Loop from Coruche suitable for beginners?
faqs.answer: No, the Loop from Coruche is generally considered a hard gravel ride. It covers approximately 87.4 kilometers with about 600 meters of elevation gain, requiring very good fitness and advanced riding skills. Some sections might even necessitate pushing your bike.

faqs.question: How long does it typically take to complete the Loop from Coruche?
faqs.answer: This challenging route is estimated to take around 7 hours and 44 minutes to complete, depending on your pace and stops.

faqs.question: What are some notable sights or highlights along the route?
faqs.answer: The route takes you through Portugal's 'cork heartland', offering unique cultural and natural experiences. You'll pass through cork oak forests and agricultural lands. The town of Coruche itself is a highlight, and you'll experience scenic stretches along a Dirt road next to the river.

faqs.question: Where can I park to access the Loop from Coruche?
faqs.answer: You can typically find parking in the town of Coruche, which serves as the starting and ending point for this loop. Look for public parking areas within the town center.

faqs.question: Is public transport available to reach Coruche for cyclists?
faqs.answer: Yes, Coruche is accessible by public transport, making it an option for cyclists traveling from nearby cities like Lisbon. Check local bus schedules for routes to Coruche.

faqs.question: What is the best time of year to gravel bike the Loop from Coruche?
faqs.answer: The region offers exceptional conditions for gravel biking. Spring and autumn are generally ideal, providing pleasant temperatures. Summers can be very hot, while winters might bring rain, affecting trail conditions.

faqs.question: Is the Loop from Coruche dog-friendly?
faqs.answer: While many rural paths in Portugal are accessible, specific regulations for dogs on this route are not explicitly stated. If bringing a dog, it's advisable to keep them on a leash, especially when passing through agricultural areas or near livestock, and ensure they are fit enough for the long distance and challenging terrain.

faqs.question: Do I need a permit or pay an entrance fee for this route?
faqs.answer: No, there are no specific permits or entrance fees required to ride the Loop from Coruche. The route primarily uses public roads and trails.

faqs.question: Are there any places to stop for food or coffee along the way?
faqs.answer: Yes, as the route passes through rural areas and the town of Coruche, you can find opportunities for traditional Portuguese food and coffee stops in charming villages. It's always a good idea to carry sufficient water and snacks, especially on longer stretches.

faqs.question: Does the Loop from Coruche intersect other well-known paths?
faqs.answer: Yes, the route also passes through sections of the Ecovia 11 (Lisboa - Badajoz), Percurso Pedestre do Cavalo e da Coruja, Caminho da Herdade de Carregais de Cima, Percurso Pedestre do Cavalo e da Coruja / Ciclovia, Caminho da Herdade da Açorda, Percurso Pedestre do Peixe e da Cegonha, Travessa do Castelo, Caminho do Monte de Vale do Gato, and Caminhos do Vale ao Montado.

waypoints.name: Dirt road next to the river
